event_text: The Dutch East India Company (VOC) ship Duyfken, under Captain Willem Janszoon, explores the western coast of Cape York Peninsula. This was the first recorded landfall by a European on Australian soil.
Dutch captain Dirk Hartog in the Eendracht makes the second recorded landfall by a European, at Dirk Hartog Island on the western coast of Australia. Leaves behind the Hartog Plate.
Dutch explorer Abel Tasman explores the west coast of Tasmania, lands on its east coast and names the island Anthoonij van Diemenslandt.

English Lieutenant James Cook's expedition in HM Bark Endeavour charts the eastern coast, and claims it for the British Crown. Australia dubbed 'terra nullius' i.e., according to the European legal precepts of the era, it was 'owned' by no-one.

The British First Fleet, led by Governor Arthur Phillip arrives in New South Wales to found first European settlement and penal colony at Sydney. Colony includes 'all the islands adjacent in the Pacific Ocean' and running westward to the 135th meridian east. This claim included the islands of New Zealand, which were administered as part of New South Wales.
George Bass and Matthew Flinders leave Sydney in the Norfolk to explore and circumnavigate Van Diemen’s Land.
Bass and Flinders return to Sydney after proving that Van Diemen’s Land is an island separated from the mainland by Bass Strait.
Matthew Flinders completes the first circumnavigation of the continent (still known as 'New Holland').
The Rum Rebellion.
Blaxland, Lawson and Wentworth cross the Blue Mountains, opening the way for colonial expansion west of Sydney.
Australia's first bank, the Bank of New South Wales, opens in Macquarie Place, Sydney (it became Westpac in 1982).
A penal colony is founded at Moreton Bay, now the city of Brisbane.
New South Wales western border is extended to 129 degrees E. Van Diemen's Land is proclaimed.
The whole of Australia is claimed as British territory. The settlement of Perth is founded. Swan River Colony is declared by Charles Fremantle for Britain.
Sydney Herald (later to become The Sydney Morning Herald) first published.
John Batman and John Pascoe Fawkner establish a settlement at Port Phillip, now the city of Melbourne.
Province of South Australia proclaimed with its western border at 132 degrees E.
Australia's first parliamentary elections held for the New South Wales Legislative Council (though voting rights are restricted to males of certain wealth or property).

Australian Colonies Government Act [1850] grants representative constitutions to New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia and Tasmania, colonies set about writing constitutions which produced democratically progressive parliaments.
Australia's first university, the University of Sydney, is founded.
Victoria separates from New South Wales.
The Victorian gold rush starts when gold is found at Summerhill Creek and Ballarat.
The Eureka Stockade.
All men over 21 years of age obtain the right to vote in South Australia.
Van Diemen's Land name changed to Tasmania.
Queensland separates from New South Wales with its western border at 141 degrees E.
Saint Mary MacKillop founds Sisters of St Joseph of the Sacred Heart.
Children of Australian Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander descent are removed from their families by Australian and State government agencies.
Overland Telegraph Line linking Darwin and Adelaide opens.
The bushranger Ned Kelly is hanged.
The opening of the Sydney-Melbourne railway.
Sir Henry Parkes delivers the Tenterfield Oration.
A severe depression hits Australia.
South Australia becomes the first Australian colony, and the second place in the world, to grant women the right to vote, as well the first Parliament in the world to allow women to stand for office.

After much public debate, the Victorian, South Australian and Tasmanian referendums are successful; the New South Wales referendum narrowly fails. Later New South Wales votes 'yes' in a second referendum, and Queensland and Western Australia also vote to join.
The first contingents from various Australian colonies are sent to South Africa to participate in the Second Boer War.
The constitution is passed by the Parliament of the United Kingdom as a schedule to the Commonwealth of Australia Constitution Act, and is given royal assent.
Australia becomes a federation on 1 January. Edmund Barton becomes Prime Minister; the 7th Earl of Hopetoun becomes Governor-General.
The first parliament met in Parliament House, Melbourne.
Immigration Restriction act was introduced- The White Australian Policy.

The Franchise Act guarantees women the right to vote in federal elections (by this stage, most states had already done this). However, it excludes most non-European ethnic groups, including Aboriginal people, unless already registered to vote on State roles.
The High Court of Australia is established with Samuel Griffith as the first Chief Justice.
Chris Watson forms the first federal Labor (minority) government.
The Dalgety proposal for the national capital is revoked, and Canberra is chosen instead.
The Northern Territory comes under Commonwealth control, being split off from South Australia.
Australian Capital Territory proclaimed.
The first national census is conducted.
The Royal Australian Navy is founded.
Walter Burley Griffin wins a design competition for the new city of Canberra.
Australian soldiers are sent to the First World War. This was first time Australians had fought under the Australian flag, as opposed to that of Britain's.
Australian troops land at Anzac Cove on the Gallipoli Peninsula.
Australia suffers heavy casualties in the Western Front Battle of the Somme.
The Labor government under Billy Hughes splits over conscription. First referendum on conscription is rejected.
Second referendum on conscription is rejected. Transcontinental railway linking Adelaide to Perth is completed.
First World War ends - 60,000 Australians dead.
Prime Minister Billy Hughes signs Treaty of Versailles: the first signing of an international treaty by Australia. Australia obtains League of Nations mandate over German New Guinea.
The airline Qantas is founded.
Edith Cowan becomes the first woman elected to an Australian parliament.
The tenth parliament is formally opened in Canberra, finalising the move to the new capital.
Labor returns to office under James Scullin. The Great Depression hits Australia.
The Sydney Harbour Bridge opens.
Victoria is devastated by the Black Friday bushfires.
Australia enters the Second World War following Germany’s invasion of Poland. The Second Australian Imperial Force is raised.
John Curtin becomes Prime Minister after Robert Menzies resigns, leading Australia through the most dangerous years of the Second World War.
Feb, Fall of Singapore. 15,000 Australians become Prisoners of War of the Japanese.
Japanese air raids on northern Australia begin with the bombing of Darwin.
Battle of the Coral Sea - United States and Royal Australian Navy halt advance of the Japanese towards Port Moresby (Australian Territory of Papua).
Battle of Kokoda Trail - Australian soldiers halt Japanese march on Port Moresby.
The United Kingdom Statute of Westminster is formally adopted by Australia. The Statute formally grants Australia the right to pass laws that conflict with UK laws.
The Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme is introduced, providing subsidised medicine to all Australians.
Japan surrenders, ending the Second World War in the Pacific.
Australia becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Minister for Immigration Arthur Calwell introduces the major post-war immigration scheme.
The Nationality and Citizenship Act is passed. Rather than being identified as subjects of Britain, the Act established Australian citizenship for people who met eligibility requirements.
Construction of the Snowy Mountains Hydro-Electric Scheme begins.
Australia signs the ANZUS treaty with the United States and New Zealand.
First nuclear test conducted in Australian territory by the United Kingdom off the coast of Western Australia.
Television in Australia is launched.
Melbourne holds the Olympics.

Robert Menzies' Commonwealth Electoral Act provided that all Indigenous Australians should have the right to enrol and vote at federal elections, removing remaining restrictions applying in QLD, WA and NT.
PM Robert Menzies announces the reintroduction of compulsory military service for men aged from 18-25 years old.
Australia changes from pounds, shillings and pence to dollars and cents on Decimal Currency Day.
The ban on the employment of married women in the Commonwealth Public Service is lifted.
The 1967 referendum changes the Constitution so Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people are counted in the population and the Commonwealth can make laws for them.
More than 200,000 people participate in the largest demonstrations in Australian history, against the Vietnam War.
Neville Bonner becomes the first Aborigine to become an Australian Member of Parliament.
Aboriginal Tent Embassy erected in response to the Coalition government's approval of exploration licences and mining tenements on reserves.
The Commonwealth Conciliation and Arbitration Commission rules that women doing the same job as men have the right to be paid the same wage.
The first Labor government since 1949 is elected under the leadership of Gough Whitlam.
The White Australia policy, established from 1901 through restrictive immigration laws, is formally dismantled.
The federal voting age is dropped from 21 to 18.
The Sydney Opera House is opened.
Darwin is devastated by Cyclone Tracy.

A constitutional crisis occurs when Malcolm Fraser blocks supply, bringing the nation to a standstill until Governor-General John Kerr dismisses Prime Minister Gough Whitlam on the 11.11.75. Fraser wins elections and becomes Prime Minister.
Advance Australia Fair becomes Australia's official national anthem.
The First Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras kicks off in Sydney.
Australian women win the right to maternity leave.
Kakadu National Park and the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park are both proclaimed.
A referendum is held in Tasmania to vote for whether or not the Franklin Dam should be built.
The Ash Wednesday fires kill 71 people.
Bob Hawke defeats Fraser and leads Labor back to government.
The Australian Dollar is floated.
Medicare is established.

The government grants the freehold title of a large area of land in central Australia, including prominent landmarks Uluru and Kata Tjuta, to the Mutitjulu people, who in turn give them a 99-year lease.

The Australia Act removes the right of appeal from State courts to the British Privy Council, making the High Court the final court of appeal in Australia. The Act also removes all remaining rights of the UK parliament to pass law for Australia. Anita Cobby murder in Sydney. Russell Street Bombing in Melbourne. Crocodile Dundee is released in Australia.

Australia celebrates its bicentenary, with large celebrations and major funding for capital works projects. The new Parliament House opens. Federal referendums on 4-year parliamentary terms, recognition of local government and other issues are defeated. Brisbane hosts World Expo '88.
Rosemary Follett (Australian Labor Party) becomes the first Chief Minister of the Australian Capital Territory and the first woman to become head of government in an Australian state or territory.
Prime Minister Bob Hawke is replaced by Paul Keating.
The High Court delivers the Mabo Decision, which rules that indigenous native title does exist. This effectively extinguishes the concept of terra nullius.
All Australian states and territories agree to introduce uniform gun laws following the deaths of 35 people in the Port Arthur massacre.
The High Court hands down the Wik Decision, which holds that indigenous native title can survive the granting of pastoral leases.
Australian soldiers are deployed to East Timor as part of the INTERFET peacekeeping force.
A referendum on changing to a republic is unsuccessful.
Howard Government introduces a Goods and Services Tax.
27th Olympic Games held in Sydney.
Australian forces deployed to War to topple Taliban for supporting Al Qaeda.
Terrorist Attacks on the United States by Al Qaeda (John Howard invokes ANZUS Treaty).
2002 Bali bombings, the deadliest act of terrorism in the history of Indonesia, killing 202 people, (including 88 Australians.
Australian military deployed to Iraq War to oust the Saddam Hussein regime for serial non-compliance with the 1991 Gulf War Peace Treaty.
Prime Minister Kevin Rudd delivers the National Apology to the Stolen Generations on behalf of the Australian Parliament.
The Global Financial Crisis intensifies after the collapse of Lehman Brothers; Australia avoids a technical recession with support from mining demand and government stimulus.
Black Saturday: Massive bushfires swept across Victoria, resulting in 173 fatalities.
Gillard becomes the first female Prime Minister.
Liberal Ken Wyatt becomes the first Aboriginal person elected to the Australian House of Representatives.
Queensland affected by major flooding followed by Cyclone Yasi.
The National Disability Insurance Scheme legislation is introduced, beginning a major reform of disability support in Australia.
Malaysia Airlines Flight MH17 is shot down over eastern Ukraine, killing 298 people, including 38 Australian citizens and residents.
The Uluru Statement from the Heart calls for a First Nations Voice to Parliament and a Makarrata Commission.
Same-sex marriage is legalised in Australia after a national postal survey and parliamentary vote.
The National Redress Scheme begins, responding to institutional child sexual abuse.
The Black Summer bushfire season begins, eventually affecting large parts of Australia.
Australia confirms its first COVID-19 case, beginning the local impact of the global pandemic.
Australia closes its international border to non-residents as part of its COVID-19 pandemic response.
Australia, the United Kingdom and the United States announce the AUKUS security partnership.
Anthony Albanese leads Labor to victory at the federal election and becomes Prime Minister.
Queen Elizabeth II dies and Charles III becomes King of Australia.
The referendum to establish an Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Voice to Parliament is defeated.
The Fair Work “right to disconnect” begins for larger employers, giving many employees protection against unreasonable after-hours work contact.
The Albanese Labor government is re-elected at the federal election.
